Lithuania is officially known as the Republic of Lithuania.
Lithuania is a country located in Europe and bordered by several countries including Latvia, Belarus, Poland, and the Russian enclave of Kaliningrad.
Lithuania covers an area of 65,300 km2 (25,200 sq mi) and has an estimated population of approximately 2,897,430 in 2025.
The capital and largest city of Lithuania is Vilnius, with other major cities including Kaunas and Klaipėda.
The main language spoken in Lithuania is Lithuanian, and the population primarily belongs to the ethnolinguistic group called Lithuanians.
Lithuania has a rich history dating back to its first unification in the 1230s by Mindaugas, who established the Grand Duchy of Lithuania on 6 July 1253.
At its height, the Grand Duchy was one of the largest countries in Europe and entered a union with Poland in 1386, forming the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.
The Commonwealth existed until its partitions between 1772 and 1795, after which most of Lithuania's territory was annexed by neighboring countries.
Lithuania regained independence in 1918, and later faced Soviet occupation before officially declaring independence from the Soviet Union on 11 March 1990.
Lithuania is a member of various international organizations including the European Union, NATO, and the United Nations.
The history of Lithuania dates back to settlements founded about 10,000 years ago.
The first people settled in Lithuania after the last Ice Age in the 10th millennium BC, engaging in hunting and gathering.
The Baltic tribes mixed with local populations and maintained trade contacts during their settlements.
Lithuania comprised culturally different regions known for early medieval burial practices, with separate linguistic, cultural, and religious identities.
The first written record of Lithuania's name dates back to 1009 AD.
Mindaugas united a large part of the Baltic tribes in the 13th century, founding the State of Lithuania and getting crowned as its first King.
The Grand Duchy of Lithuania rose to prominence, reaching ~650,000 km2 in territory in the first half of the 14th century.
In 1385, Lithuania formed a union with Poland through the Union of Krewo, which later evolved into the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth.
The Commonwealth ensured equal rights for Lithuania and Poland and significantly influenced the region's politics and military affairs.
Lithuania suffered military losses during the mid-17th century, leading to temporary foreign occupations and annexations.
By the late 18th century, the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth was partitioned three times, leading to the disappearance of both independent Lithuania and Poland from the political map in 1795.
The history of Lithuania begins with settlements founded about 10,000 years ago.
The first inhabitants were travelers who engaged in hunting and gathering.
By the 3rd-2nd millennium BC, the Baltic tribes mixed with local populations and formed distinct cultural identities.
Lithuanians attempted to restore their statehood during uprisings in 1830-1831 and again in 1863-1864, but both were suppressed by Russian forces leading to stricter Russification measures.
Significant figures like S. Daukantas and M. Narbutt contributed to the revival of Lithuanian culture and identity.
The first significant gathering aimed at restoring Lithuanian sovereignty occurred in 1905, demanding wider autonomy for Lithuania.
Lithuania declared independence in 1918, establishing itself as a sovereign state after World War I.
The country faced numerous challenges, including territorial disputes with Poland and eventual incorporation into Poland in 1922.
In 1940, Lithuania was occupied by the Soviet Union, leading to significant repression and attempts to restore independence during World War II.
After World War II, Lithuania faced Soviet re-occupation, leading to resistance movements that were ultimately suppressed.
The independence movement gained traction in the late 1980s, culminating in a significant event in 1989.
On 11 March 1990, Lithuania announced the restoration of its independence, becoming the first Soviet-occupied state to do so.
The Act of independence led to Soviet blockades and shortages in Lithuania.
Tensions escalated in January 1991 with a failed coup attempt by Soviet forces, resulting in casualties during the defense of independence.
Lithuania joined the United Nations on 17 September 1991 and adopted its current constitution through a referendum on 25 October 1992.
Lithuania's first president after independence was elected on 14 February 1993.
Lithuania joined NATO in March 2004 and the European Union on 1 May 2004.
Lithuania has a temperate climate influenced by both maritime and continental air masses, with significant seasonal temperature variations.
Lithuania's landscape includes moderate lowlands, highlands, diverse terrain with lakes, forests, and wetlands.
The country has thousands of lakes and features the highest drifting sand dunes in Europe located near the Curonian Spit.

Aim to cut emissions by 20% of 1990 levels by 2020 and 40% by 2030
At least 20% of Lithuania's total energy consumption from renewable sources by 2020, 27% by 2030

Approximately 29,000 rivers with a total length of 64,000 km

Strong democratic traditions established since independence
First independent general elections held on 25 October 1992
Current structure of government includes a directly elected president and parliament (Seimas)
Current president oversees foreign affairs, national security, and military
Judicial appointments made by the president and parliament in Lithuania
Political parties must receive at least 5% of the national vote to secure parliament seats
Lithuania was one of the first countries to grant women the right to vote, which they utilized in the elections of 1919.
Lithuania allowed women to vote earlier than several other democratic countries: Sweden (1920), Italy (1945), Switzerland (1952), and Japan (1971).
Lithuania has a fragmented multi-party system, with coalition governments being common.
Ordinary elections to the parliament are held every four years on the second Sunday of October.
Candidates for parliament must be at least 21 years old, not under allegiance to a foreign state, and must permanently reside in Lithuania.
Persons with court-imposed sentences and certain officials are not eligible to stand for election.
The prime minister can be confirmed following coalition agreements in parliament.
The president is elected for a five-year term through majority vote, with a maximum of two terms.
Municipalities in Lithuania are governed by a mayor and a municipal council, with council members elected on a four-year term.
Mayors are directly elected by residents since 2015, while council members are elected via proportional representation.
As of 2023, 1,498 municipal council members were elected, and one party won the most positions in these elections.
Ordinary elections for the European Parliament allow citizens of Lithuania and EU citizens residing in Lithuania to vote, with eligibility criteria set for candidates.
The first attempt to codify Lithuanian laws occurred in 1468 with the compilation of a legal code by Grand Duke.
Three editions of the Statute were created in the 16th century: the First Statute in 1529, the Second Statute in 1566, and the Third Statute in 1588.
On 3 May 1791, the Constitution of 3 May was adopted, making it Europe's first and the world's second constitution.
The Third Statute remained partly in force in Lithuania until 1840, despite the partition of Lithuania in 1795.
After regaining independence in 1990, modified Soviet legal codes were in effect for about a decade until the current constitution was adopted on 25 October 1992.
The 2001 Civil Code was passed, later succeeded by the Civil and Commercial Code in 2003.
The Lithuanian legal system is characterized by formality and rationalisation as opposed to practicality and informality.
Normative legal acts come into force the day after publication in the Legal Acts Register unless otherwise specified.
The Lithuanian legal system has integrated the principles of the European Union since 1 May 2004.
Law enforcement agencies, including the Lithuanian Police, have worked to reduce crime in Lithuania since independence.
Crime rates in Lithuania have been declining, with notable decreases in thefts and serious crimes reported in 2017.
29% of Lithuanians perceive that corruption affects their daily lives, indicating a significant concern about corruption in their country.
Lithuania has one of the highest rates of prison inmates in the EU, attributed to high repression levels rather than high criminality.
